panel.origin: A close-range shot, usually banked softly off the backboard or laid over the rim, taken while moving toward the basket.
panel.mixology: She drove the lane, absorbed the contact, and still kissed the layup off the glass — the most basic shot in the game, made to look effortless.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the shooter 'lays the ball up' gently toward the hoop. It's the first shot most players ever learn.

panel.origin: A field goal scored from behind the three-point arc, worth three points instead of two — the shot that reshaped modern basketball.
panel.mixology: Down two with seconds left, he stepped back behind the line and let the three-pointer fly; the gym held its breath as it arced toward the rim.
panel.examples: The three-point line was adopted by the NBA in 1979 after experiments in the old ABA; decades later, analytics made it the centerpiece of strategy.

panel.origin: A two-player action where one sets a screen ('pick') for the ball-handler, then cuts ('rolls') to the basket for a pass.
panel.mixology: They ran the pick-and-roll to perfection all night — the screen freeing the guard, the big man rolling to the rim, the defense always a step behind.
panel.examples: Descriptive of its two phases. Often called basketball's most fundamental play, it has anchored offenses from the playground to the pros for generations.

panel.origin: A pass thrown near the rim for a teammate to catch in mid-air and finish with a dunk or layup before landing.
panel.mixology: The lob hung in the air for an impossible beat before he rose, caught it, and slammed the alley-oop home as the bench exploded.
panel.examples: From the French 'allez, hop!' — a cry used by circus acrobats and trapeze artists before a leap, borrowed for the airborne finish.

panel.origin: Clearing out a side of the court so one player can go one-on-one against a single defender, isolating the matchup.
panel.mixology: Late in the clock they cleared out for an iso, handing the ball to their best scorer and letting four teammates simply watch the duel unfold.
panel.examples: Short for 'isolation,' the term grew as star-driven offenses leaned on individual scorers to beat their defender in open space.

panel.origin: Scoring by jumping and forcing the ball directly down through the hoop with one or both hands — basketball's most emphatic play.
panel.mixology: He caught the lob and threw down a thunderous dunk, hanging on the rim a beat too long as the crowd lost its collective mind.
panel.examples: From 'dunk,' to dip something into liquid — the motion of pushing the ball down into the basket. It was actually banned in college play through the 1970s.

panel.origin: A dribble move that quickly switches the ball from one hand to the other to fake a defender out of position.
panel.mixology: His crossover was lethal; one hard dribble left, then a snap back right, and the defender's ankles were left tangled on the hardwood.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the ball 'crosses over' between hands. The move's flashiest form, the 'ankle-breaker,' became an icon of 1990s playground and pro style.

panel.origin: A soft, high-arcing shot lofted over taller defenders near the basket before they can contest it. Also called a 'teardrop.'
panel.mixology: Too small to challenge the giant in the paint, the guard floated a teardrop over his outstretched arms, the ball dropping in untouched.
panel.examples: Named for the way the ball seems to 'float' over the defense. It's the small player's classic answer to shot-blockers guarding the rim.

panel.origin: Establishing position with your back to the basket near the low post, using size and footwork to score close in.
panel.mixology: The big man posted up on the block, backed his defender down with two dribbles, and finished with a smooth turnaround over his shoulder.
panel.examples: From 'the post,' the area near the basket. Once the dominant way big men scored, the post-up has faded somewhat in the three-point era.

panel.origin: A defensive scheme where each defender guards one specific opponent, following them across the court.
panel.mixology: They switched to man-to-man in the fourth, every defender locked onto a man, turning the game into five individual battles of will.
panel.examples: Descriptive — each defender takes one man. It's the foundational defense taught before players learn the team-oriented complexities of zone.

panel.origin: A defensive scheme where players guard areas of the court rather than specific opponents, packing space to deter drives.
panel.mixology: The 2-3 zone clogged the paint completely, forcing the offense into rushed jumpers all night and daring them to beat it from outside.
panel.examples: Descriptive — defenders cover 'zones' of the floor. The NBA actually banned zone defense for decades to keep the game flowing before reintroducing it in 2001.

panel.origin: Deflecting or stopping an opponent's shot in mid-air without fouling — one of defense's most dramatic plays. Also a 'rejection.'
panel.mixology: He timed the leap perfectly and swatted the layup off the backboard, a rejection so emphatic the shooter just stood and stared.
panel.examples: From the general sense of 'to block,' to obstruct. The spectacular swat-into-the-stands version is showy; purists prize the controlled block that keeps possession.

panel.origin: Taking the ball away from the offense, by picking off a pass or knocking it loose from a dribbler.
panel.mixology: She read the lazy cross-court pass, jumped the lane for the steal, and was gone the other way before anyone could react.
panel.examples: From 'to steal,' to take what isn't yours. It's the defensive playmaker's signature stat, the reward for anticipation and quick hands.

panel.origin: Using your body to seal off an opponent and carve out space for a rebound as a shot goes up.
panel.mixology: He didn't grab a single highlight, but his relentless boxing out gave his guards the rebounds that quietly won the game.
panel.examples: From 'boxing out' an opponent — putting your body like a box between them and the ball. It's the unglamorous fundamental behind every rebound.

panel.origin: An aggressive defense applied across the entire court, pressuring the offense the moment they inbound to force turnovers.
panel.mixology: Trailing late, they sprang the full-court press, swarming the inbounds pass and forcing a panicked turnover that swung the whole game.
panel.examples: Descriptive — the 'press' extends the full length of the court. Famously weaponized by coaches as a comeback tool and a way to exhaust opponents.

panel.origin: When a defender leaves their own assignment to assist a teammate who's been beaten, then recovers.
panel.mixology: Beaten off the dribble, he trusted his teammate's help defense; sure enough, the weak-side big rotated over to wall off the rim.
panel.examples: Descriptive — a defender comes to 'help.' Good help defense is the hallmark of a connected team, turning five individuals into a single shifting wall.

panel.origin: The primary ball-handler and floor leader, responsible for running the offense and setting up teammates. The 'one.'
panel.mixology: The point guard surveyed the floor like a chess player, calling the play, probing the defense, and slipping a no-look pass to the open shooter.
panel.examples: From 'point,' the position at the top of the offense from which a guard 'points' the team's attack. Traditionally the smallest, quickest player.

panel.origin: The painted rectangular area beneath each basket — also the 'key' or 'lane' — where much of the game's contact happens.
panel.mixology: He owned the paint all night, swatting drives and grabbing rebounds; in that cramped, physical space, his size simply ruled.
panel.examples: Named because the area is literally painted a solid color on the court. The related 'key' refers to its old keyhole shape in earlier eras of the game.

panel.origin: The team's best substitute, first off the bench, often a scorer who provides an instant spark.
panel.mixology: He embraced the sixth-man role, entering with the second unit and lighting up tired defenses with a burst of scoring off the bench.
panel.examples: Named because they are effectively the sixth starter — the first player off the bench beyond the five on the floor. The NBA gives a dedicated award for it.

panel.origin: Traditionally the tallest player, stationed near the basket to score inside, rebound, and protect the rim. The 'five.'
panel.mixology: The center anchored everything, a towering presence who altered every shot in the paint and cleaned the glass at both ends.
panel.examples: Named for the position's traditional role at the 'center' of action near the hoop. The role has evolved as modern centers stretch out to shoot threes.

panel.origin: The three-point line itself — the curved boundary beyond which made shots count for three. Also 'beyond the arc.'
panel.mixology: Spotting up beyond the arc, she caught and released in one motion; the analytics era has made that spot the most valuable real estate on the floor.
panel.examples: Named for its curved, arc-like shape on the court. 'From beyond the arc' has become shorthand for the entire three-point revolution in the modern game.

panel.origin: The boundary line running under each basket, along the ends of the court. Players use it as an extra 'defender' to trap dribblers.
panel.mixology: She drove the baseline and reversed it under the hoop, using the line itself as cover before laying it in on the far side.
panel.examples: Descriptive — it forms the 'base' line at each end of the court. A defender who steers a dribbler toward it can trap them with nowhere to go.

panel.origin: Pushing the ball up the floor quickly after a rebound or steal to score before the defense can set up.
panel.mixology: The rebound triggered a fast break, three players streaking downcourt in a blur, the outlet pass finding the leader for an easy two.
panel.examples: Descriptive — a 'fast' attack that 'breaks' away from the defense before it recovers. The thrilling antidote to slow, set half-court basketball.

panel.origin: A balanced stance, ball in hand, from which a player can immediately shoot, pass, or dribble — keeping the defense guessing.
panel.mixology: He caught the ball in triple-threat position, and the defender froze, unable to commit because all three options were equally live.
panel.examples: Named for the three simultaneous 'threats' — shoot, pass, drive — the stance preserves. It's a cornerstone of fundamental offensive teaching.

panel.origin: A legal block set by an offensive player to free a teammate from their defender. The same action as a 'pick.'
panel.mixology: The screen caught the defender flush, and for a split second the shooter was wide open — all the daylight a great shooter ever needs.
panel.examples: From 'to screen,' to shield or block from view — the screener's body shields the teammate from the defender. 'Pick' is the more informal synonym.

panel.origin: Passing to a teammate and immediately cutting to the basket to receive a return pass for an easy score.
panel.mixology: Classic give-and-go: he hit the post, cut hard to the rim, and the ball came right back for a layup before the defense even turned its head.
panel.examples: Descriptive of its rhythm — you 'give' the ball, then 'go' to the basket. One of the oldest two-man plays in the sport, predating the modern game.

panel.origin: Sending two defenders to guard a single ball-handler, usually to trap a star scorer or force the ball out of their hands.
panel.mixology: They double-teamed him the instant he touched the ball, two defenders swarming, betting that anyone else beating them was a risk worth taking.
panel.examples: Descriptive — two defenders 'team up' on one. The strategic gamble is that leaving someone else open is the lesser evil against an elite scorer.

panel.origin: The phase when possession changes and a team moves quickly from defense to offense, before the opposing defense is set.
panel.mixology: They were lethal in transition, turning every stop into a sprint the other way, scoring before the defense could even find its assignments.
panel.examples: From 'transition,' the change from one state to another — here, from defense to offense. Modern pace-and-space basketball thrives in these chaotic moments.

panel.origin: A badly missed shot that clangs hard off the rim or backboard, as ugly and heavy as a thrown brick.
panel.mixology: He threw up an air-balling brick at the worst possible moment, and the home crowd let him hear about it with a chorus of groans.
panel.examples: Slang likening a clanging miss to a brick — heavy, hard, and graceless. The crowd chant 'brick! brick!' to rattle a struggling free-throw shooter is a staple.

panel.origin: Being fouled while making a shot, earning the basket plus one free throw — a three-point play from a two-point bucket.
panel.mixology: He finished through the contact and roared 'and-one!' as the whistle blew, the chance to complete a three-point play already in his eyes.
panel.examples: Short for 'the basket and one free throw.' The triumphant shout has become a celebration in itself, even lending its name to a streetball brand.

panel.origin: Slang for a long three-point shot, as if launched from far away — 'from downtown.'
panel.mixology: He pulled up from well beyond the line and drained it — 'from downtown!' the announcer howled, the call as old as the three itself.
panel.examples: Broadcaster slang evoking a shot from way out, 'downtown,' far from the basket. It became a signature call of the three-point era's growing showmanship.

panel.origin: To dunk dramatically over a defender, humiliating them so memorably the moment belongs on a poster.
panel.mixology: He rose over the helpless defender and posterized him with a one-handed slam, an image destined for every highlight reel that night.
panel.examples: From the literal posters such dunks became — the dunker immortalized, the defender frozen beneath. A modern coinage of the highlight-and-social-media age.

panel.origin: A shot released just before the game or quarter clock expires that counts if it leaves the hand in time — the ultimate dramatic finish.
panel.mixology: The buzzer-beater banked in as time hit zero, and the arena erupted; one shot, half a second, and a season changed forever.
panel.examples: Named for beating the 'buzzer' that signals the end of a period. Replay review now settles the agonizing question of whether the ball left the hand in time.

panel.origin: Recording double-digit totals in three statistical categories in one game — usually points, rebounds, and assists.
panel.mixology: He notched a triple-double by the third quarter — points, boards, and dimes all in double figures — a stat line that screams all-around dominance.
panel.examples: Descriptive — three categories reaching 'double' digits. The rarer 'quadruple-double' adds a fourth, a feat achieved only a handful of times in NBA history.

panel.origin: A perfect made shot that drops cleanly through the net without touching the rim — pure and satisfying.
panel.mixology: Nothing but net — the swish so clean the net snapped up and the shooter was already backpedaling on defense, certain it was good.
panel.examples: Onomatopoeic, imitating the soft 'swish' sound of the ball passing through the net untouched. The sound itself has become shorthand for a perfect shot.
